text: The surgeon enters the abdomen through an incision in the left upper quadrant, initiating the surgical procedure.
The surgeon places bilateral 8mm ports in the upper quadrants for instrumentation and a 12mm camera port in the midline for visualization.
The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap and extends it towards the symptomatic left side, preparing the area for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon dissects laterally towards the psoas muscle and inferiorly towards Cooper's ligament, revealing these anatomical structures during the robotic-assisted inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon performs further dissection, revealing a traumatic disruption in the entire left groin area and a midline incisional hernia.
The surgeon identifies the chord structures and carefully separates them from the peritoneum, enhancing the understanding of the hernia anatomy.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the myopic tineal orifice, creating space for the placement of surgical mesh.
The camera shows the fully dissected space, providing a clear view of the anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the flap dissection, extending towards the right side of the abdomen, reaching the psoas muscles and Cooper's ligament.
The surgeon creates a large peritoneal flap, visible within the dissected space, facilitating further surgical access and preparation for mesh placement.
The surgeon identifies a small right-sided inguinal hernia and a cord lipoma within the dissected space, visible medial to the lateral umbilical fold.
The surgeon reduces the hernia sac and continues the dissection of the peritoneal flap, carefully separating it from the cord structures.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the preperitoneal flap, revealing the full extent of the right-sided inguinal hernia, with clear visualization of the hernia sac and surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon exposes the entire left-sided myopectenial orifice, revealing three hernias, including a ventral incisional hernia, within the surgical field.
The surgeon closes the lower midline incisional hernia using an O-stratofix suture in a running fashion, securing the tissue edges together.
The surgeon uses an O-stratofix suture to close and repair the traumatic hernia and left inguinal disruption, ensuring the tissue edges are approximated.
The surgeon takes superficial bites of muscle and fascia, carefully avoiding nerve entrapment during the closure of the hernia repair.
The surgeon has closed the left myopectenial orifice, which now visually resembles normal anatomical structure.
The video displays a side-by-side comparison of the left inguinal region, showing the anatomical appearance before and after the closure of hernias.
The video shows the re-approximated defects in the left inguinal region, with the surgical repair appearing well-aligned and stable.
The newly closed surface of the left inguinal region is visible, providing a stable area for the placement of surgical mesh.
Two elliptically cut meshes are placed in the surgical field, overlapping at the midline to ensure adequate coverage.
The surgeon reinforces the mesh in the lower midline and secures it with Vicryl sutures, ensuring stable placement over the surgical site.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap using a running 2-0 V-lock absorbable suture, extending from the central portion of the flap to both sides, effectively covering the entire mesh.
The surgeon desufflates the abdomen and removes all laparoscopic ports after confirming the closure is satisfactory.
